# ServerMind

> ServerMind is a free, open-source, self-hosted AI assistant for managing one Linux server (VPS) — or a whole fleet of them — through chat. You ask it questions in plain English — "Is Redis healthy?", "Why is nginx down?", "Restart api-prod", "How much disk is left?", "Which servers are unhealthy?" — and it picks the right tool, runs it, and explains the result. It also serves a live dashboard (CPU, memory, disk, uptime, PM2 processes, service health) for a single server or every server in a fleet. You bring your own AI: the free Google Gemini tier, a Claude subscription, or any OpenAI-compatible API (Groq, OpenRouter, or local Ollama).

## Key facts

- **License:** MIT (open-source, no telemetry, no cloud backend).
- **Cost:** Free. The only optional cost is a paid AI provider, but the default Google Gemini tier is free (~1,500 requests/day, no credit card).
- **Runs on:** A Linux VPS (Ubuntu, Debian, Fedora, Alpine). Built with Bun + Hono (TypeScript); no build step.
- **Install:** One command — `curl -fsSL https://servermind.dev/install.sh | bash`. The installer sets up Bun and PM2, then a setup wizard configures auth, AI backend, and services. About two minutes.
- **Domain required:** No. It binds to localhost; reach it privately over an SSH tunnel or Tailscale. A public HTTPS domain (via Caddy or Nginx) is optional.

## Security model (enforced server-side, not by trusting the AI)

- **No raw shell.** Every command runs as argv against a strict read-only allowlist (df, free, ps, ss, systemctl status, journalctl, reading logs under /var/log), so command injection is structurally impossible. Deletes, file writes, and network access are not in the allowlist.
- **Mutations are gated.** Service restart/stop/start are refused unless the operator flips a server-side "arm" switch that the AI cannot set itself. The gate is re-checked on every call, an arm auto-expires after a few minutes, and a single change consumes it — so prompt injection in tool output cannot silently flip the switch or chain extra changes.
- **Authentication.** Password + TOTP two-factor auth, argon2id hashing, HttpOnly/SameSite=Strict session cookies, brute-force lockout, rate limiting, strict Content-Security-Policy.
- **Privacy.** Secrets stay in local files (chmod 600). Only your chosen AI provider receives your prompt and the command output it needs, under your own account. Use local Ollama for zero data egress.
- **No database.** Settings are a JSON file and sessions are in memory — there is no SQL/ORM, so SQL injection has no surface to attack.
- **Settings store.** A dashboard Settings panel edits a safe subset (email, alerts, monitoring, AI) live; it cannot change auth, the service allowlist, or PM2 access. Writes are schema-validated (Zod) and atomic; secret fields (SMTP password, API keys) are encrypted at rest with AES-256-GCM, with the key kept in .env.

## What it manages

PM2 processes, Redis, MySQL/MariaDB, Nginx, Caddy, systemd services, Docker, fail2ban, plus disk, memory, ports, and logs.

## Custom tools

Beyond the built-in allowlist, an operator can add their own tools from the dashboard's Tools tab (operator-only, behind auth) — without writing code or weakening the safety model. Five kinds: (1) a pinned command — one exact, frozen argv run with no shell (read-only by default, optionally a mutation gated by the arm switch); (2) a read-only DB query — a frozen SQL statement (MySQL/MariaDB or PostgreSQL); (3) a DB console — the AI writes a read-only SELECT at call time against a configured database, so you can ask "how many active orders?" in plain English; (4) an HTTP health check — GET a frozen URL with an optional status/JSON assertion; (5) read a file — a frozen path confined to safe roots. The core principle: the operator defines and freezes the tool, and the AI can only trigger it (it never supplies a command, path, or URL — the one exception is the DB console, where it writes a query that is validated first). Database tools enforce read-only two ways: a statement gate (only SELECT/SHOW/EXPLAIN-class, single statement, no INTO OUTFILE / LOAD_FILE / COPY … PROGRAM / pg_read_file vectors) plus, for PostgreSQL, an engine-enforced read-only transaction that rejects any write including data-modifying CTEs. The strongest boundary is the database user's grants, so DB tools should point at a least-privilege SELECT-only role. Connection passwords are encrypted at rest (AES-256-GCM) and masked in the API. Mutating commands stay behind the same server-side arm switch as a service restart. On a fleet, custom tools are agent-owned: each server defines its own tools and advertises only their names to the controller; when you manage that server the call runs ON that box and is re-validated locally, so the controller can trigger a server's tools but never define or push one. Details: https://servermind.dev/docs.html#custom-tools

## Multiple servers (fleet)

A single server runs zero-config on its own. To manage many, one instance is designated a controller (set FLEET_JOIN_TOKEN) and the other servers run a lightweight agent that dials OUT to the controller over a single WebSocket — so no new inbound ports are opened on the agents. Install is one command with role flags: the controller is `curl -fsSL https://servermind.dev/install.sh | bash`, and each agent is `curl -fsSL https://servermind.dev/install.sh | bash -s -- --controller wss://<controller>/fleet/agent --token <token>`. The controller shows every server in one dashboard under one login, with fleet-wide AI chat ("which servers are unhealthy?") and reports/alerts deduped into one inbox. Security is per server: each agent enforces its own read-only allowlist and arm switch, so the controller can ask but cannot bypass a server's guardrails. Fleet-wide chat requires an OpenAI-compatible backend on the controller (the Claude Code backend is local-only). Setup guide: https://servermind.dev/docs.html#fleet

## Self-hosted WireGuard mesh (no third party)

Optionally, the controller↔agent link can run over a self-hosted WireGuard mesh — add `--mesh` to both install commands. The controller installs WireGuard, opens UDP 51820, and writes a tightly-scoped sudoers rule so the unprivileged app can reload only its own WireGuard interface (no shell, no other command). Each agent generates its OWN WireGuard keypair locally — its private key never leaves the box — enrolls with the controller by sending only its public key, brings up its tunnel, then talks to the controller over the encrypted mesh. There is no third-party coordination server (unlike Tailscale); every key is generated on your own machines. Agents are added and revoked from the Fleet dashboard's "Add server" button, which prints the exact one-line enroll command. The shared join token is used only for the first bootstrap hop — the WireGuard keys themselves are per-agent and individually revocable.

## Email reports & alerts

ServerMind can email a daily health report and send alerts when disk or memory crosses a threshold, a monitored service goes down, or a TLS certificate is near expiry. It relays through SMTP (e.g. a Gmail app password) or the Resend API — it does not run its own mail server. Configured in the setup wizard.

## Common questions

- **Is it free?** Yes — MIT open-source; free with the Google Gemini tier.
- **Do I need a domain?** No — SSH tunnel or Tailscale needs no domain or TLS.
- **Do I need Caddy or Nginx?** No — only for an optional public HTTPS domain.
- **Is it safe to let AI control my server?** Yes — read-only allowlist plus a server-side arm switch; destructive commands are impossible.
- **Can it manage multiple servers?** Yes — one controller + lightweight agents that dial out; monitor and manage the whole fleet from one login. A single server needs none of this.
- **Can I add my own tools?** Yes — from the dashboard Tools tab you can add custom tools (a frozen command, a read-only DB query, a DB console the AI writes SELECTs against, an HTTP check, or a file to read). You freeze them; the AI can only trigger them. No code, and the safety model is unchanged.
- **Which AI should I use?** Gemini (free), Claude (best quality), Ollama (private/offline), or Groq (fast/free).

## Alternatives & comparison

ServerMind is comparable to OpenClaw (an autonomous agent over Telegram/Discord), Teleport Assist (enterprise access platform with AI chat), Portainer MCP (Docker-focused, via an MCP client), Netdata (heavy monitoring platform with AI chat), Cockpit/Webmin (web config dashboards without AI), and Warp (an AI terminal on your laptop). ServerMind's distinguishing combination is: self-hosted + web dashboard + conversational AI + safety guardrails (a server-side arm switch plus a read-only command allowlist) + no domain required. Full side-by-side comparison: https://servermind.dev/compare.html

## Links

- Home: https://servermind.dev/
- Setup guide: https://servermind.dev/docs.html
- Source code (GitHub): https://github.com/AjjlalAhmed/servermind
- Install script: https://servermind.dev/install.sh
